input.background: In 1954, the prominent statistician W. G. Cochran (1909-1980) published an important article on the analysis of cross-tabulated counts. On page 435 of the article, techniques were illustrated with an example from a study on the treatment of leprosy. Table 18.10 lists the cross-tabulated results. The row variable in this table classifies patients according to their initial degree of skin damage. The column variable classifies overall response to therapy.
input.question: Address whether patients with high levels of skin damage progressed differently than those with low levels of skin damage by calculating the conditional percents of improvement in the two groups.

output.answer:
Here are the relevant conditional distributions:

Skin infiltration:
- High: 13% marked improvement, 29% slight improvement, 31% stationary, 25% worse, 2% total.
- Low: 8% marked improvement, 19% slight improvement, 29% stationary, 37% worse, 8% total.

These distributions show that patients with high skin damage were more likely to show improvement than those with low skin infiltration.

Skin infiltration.description: The initial degree of skin damage in patients.

Marked improvement.description: Number of patients showing marked improvement in response to therapy.

Moderate improvement.description: Number of patients showing moderate improvement in response to therapy.

Slight improvement.description: Number of patients showing slight improvement in response to therapy.

Stationary.description: Number of patients whose condition remained stationary.

Worse.description: Number of patients whose condition worsened.

Total.description: Total number of patients in each skin infiltration group.
